construct_name: Soap Note: Pharmacy Tech Verbal Escalation
construct_description: Call the on-duty pharmacist to flag two prescription concerns before filling them. Demonstrate use of ISMP verbal escalation format — Identify yourself, Patient ID, Concern, Specific issue, Recommendation/Ask — to communicate drug interaction and allergy risks clearly.

scene.title: Prescription Flags — Pharmacist Escalation Call
scene.context: You are a pharmacy technician and you have just pulled up David Park's prescriptions for filling. You've identified two prescriptions in the queue that have flags you are not authorized to override on your own. You need to call the on-duty pharmacist before dispensing. The pharmacist answers — give your verbal escalation using the ISMP format. Be specific: name the drugs, name the concerns, and tell the pharmacist what you need them to do.
scene.urgency_label: Hold on dispensing — 2 flags pending review

reference_sections.label: Patient Profile
reference_sections.content_html:
NameDavid Park
Age / Sex72-year-old male
MRNMRN-447821
DOB03/14/1953
AllergiesCodeine (nausea/vomiting), Latex

reference_sections.label: Current Medications
reference_sections.content_html:
DrugDose / Sig
Warfarin5 mg daily
Lisinopril10 mg daily
Metoprolol succinate25 mg BID
Atorvastatin20 mg daily (evening)
Omeprazole20 mg daily

reference_sections.label: New Prescriptions — Flagged
reference_sections.content_html:
Rx #1: Tramadol 50 mg TID PRN pain

  • Tramadol + warfarin → increased bleeding risk
  • Tramadol is an opioid-like compound → potential cross-sensitivity with codeine allergy

Rx #2: Fluconazole 150 mg single dose

  • Fluconazole is a strong CYP2C9 inhibitor
  • Warfarin is metabolized via CYP2C9 → INR can spike dramatically
  • Potential for serious hemorrhagic event if not managed

reference_sections.label: ISMP Escalation Format
reference_sections.content_html:
1. Identify yourself — name, role, location
  2. Patient ID — name, DOB or MRN
  3. Concern #1 — drug name, specific issue, clinical risk
  4. Concern #2 — drug name, specific issue, clinical risk
  5. Ask — what do you need the pharmacist to do?
  6. Urgency — clarify dispensing is on hold pending review

sections.label: Identify Yourself
sections.prompt: State your name, your role (pharmacy technician), and where you're calling from.

sections.label: Patient Identification
sections.prompt: Give patient name, date of birth or MRN so the pharmacist can pull the chart.

sections.label: Concern
sections.prompt: State that you have flagged prescriptions you need reviewed before dispensing.

sections.label: Specific Issues
sections.prompt: Name each drug and explain the specific concern clearly — interaction, allergy risk, clinical consequence.

sections.label: Recommendation / Ask
sections.prompt: Tell the pharmacist what you need: DUR override review, prescriber contact, clinical check. Note that dispensing is on hold.

required_elements.label: Self-identification as pharmacy technician
required_elements.teaching_point: Lead with who you are. The pharmacist needs to immediately understand your scope of practice — you are a tech escalating for pharmacist oversight, not making a clinical recommendation. Starting without identification is an ISMP-flagged communication failure.

required_elements.label: Patient identification (name and age/DOB/MRN)
required_elements.teaching_point: Two patient identifiers are required before any medication discussion (Joint Commission NPSG 01.01.01). Name plus DOB or MRN confirms you're both looking at the same patient record — this prevents errors from lookaliike or soundalike names.

required_elements.label: Tramadol concern (interaction and/or allergy)
required_elements.teaching_point: Two independent concerns exist with tramadol: (1) it potentiates warfarin's anticoagulant effect, elevating bleeding risk; and (2) tramadol has opioid-like activity and the patient has a documented codeine allergy — cross-sensitivity is a real clinical consideration. Naming both shows deeper safety awareness.

required_elements.label: Fluconazole concern (CYP2C9 / warfarin INR)
required_elements.teaching_point: Fluconazole is one of the most clinically significant warfarin interactions in pharmacy practice. It inhibits CYP2C9, the primary enzyme that clears warfarin, causing INR to spike to dangerous levels. Naming the mechanism (CYP2C9 inhibition) rather than just 'an interaction' demonstrates clinical depth.

required_elements.label: Specific ask to the pharmacist
required_elements.teaching_point: Close with a clear action request. 'I need you to review the DUR flags' or 'Can you contact the prescriber about the fluconazole?' defines the pharmacist's role. Escalating without a specific ask puts the burden on them to figure out what you need — that slows resolution.

required_elements.label: Dispensing on hold pending review
required_elements.teaching_point: Explicitly stating 'I have not dispensed these yet' closes the loop on safety. It confirms the medication has not left the pharmacy, which is the entire point of the escalation. Without this statement, the pharmacist cannot be certain the drugs aren't already on their way to the patient.

performance_labels.label: Exemplary
performance_labels.description: Full ISMP protocol followed. Both concerns named with mechanisms. Clear ask made. Dispensing hold confirmed. Pharmacist has everything needed to act immediately.

performance_labels.label: Proficient
performance_labels.description: Both drugs mentioned with concerns. May be missing mechanism detail (e.g., named interaction but not CYP2C9) or dispensing hold not explicitly stated.

performance_labels.label: Developing
performance_labels.description: One concern communicated well, second is thin or missing. Or the ask is vague ('I just wanted to let you know').

performance_labels.label: Beginning
performance_labels.description: Patient identified but clinical concerns are not specific. Pharmacist would need to ask clarifying questions before acting.

performance_labels.label: Insufficient
performance_labels.description: Report does not give the pharmacist enough information to identify the safety concern or take action.
